benchmarks.ours: Top 5% of members = 20.5% of allowed spend; $5,280 vs $1,150 PMPM.
benchmarks.national: Nationally, the top 5% of people account for ~50% of all U.S. health spending (50.8% in 2015); the bottom 50% just 2.8%.
benchmarks.implication: Concentration is structural, not anomalous. A high-cost claimant care pod is the highest-ROI lever — our 20.5% understates the ceiling because our book excludes the uninsured/institutionalized.

benchmarks.ours: 76% of ED visits avoidable/potentially avoidable; ED $1,127 vs urgent care $135 (8x).
benchmarks.national: 155 million U.S. ED visits in 2022; ~10% triaged non-urgent; highest visit rate among adults 75+ (76 per 100).
benchmarks.implication: ED overuse and an elderly skew are national patterns — our 65+ hotspot mirrors the data. Triage lines + urgent-care/telehealth routing are proven diversion levers.

benchmarks.ours: Specialty Rx $791K; oncology biologics 43%; GLP-1 a top class.
benchmarks.national: U.S. retail Rx spending reached $467.0B in 2024 (+7.9%); projected fastest-growing NHE category (5.7%/yr, 2025-34); diabetes/obesity (GLP-1) drugs a key driver.
benchmarks.implication: Specialty/GLP-1 is the steepest national cost curve. PA refinement, biosimilar & site-of-care shifts protect SummitBridge against a trend that is accelerating faster than any other category.

benchmarks.ours: Inpatient = 76% of our allowed spend ($8.6M); the single largest bucket.
benchmarks.national: ~1 in 5 Medicare patients are readmitted within 30 days; MedPAC estimated ~75% of 30-day readmissions are potentially preventable (~$12B in Medicare spend).
benchmarks.implication: The Phase-2 prize. Inpatient dwarfs every quick win; national evidence says most 30-day readmissions are preventable. Discharge planning + transitional care target the largest, most-addressable bucket.

benchmarks.ours: 11.6% disenrollment; leavers 36% higher PMPM; 'Premium Affordability' the #1 exit reason.
benchmarks.national: CMS projects ~4.7M direct-purchase (Marketplace) enrollees will drop when enhanced subsidies expire in 2026; insured share falls from 91.8% (2024) toward 90.5% (2034).
benchmarks.implication: Affordability pressure is systemic and about to intensify. Cost-transparency tools + proactive retention outreach to high-PMPM members protect the book precisely as subsidy-driven affordability shocks hit.
